steps.prompt: Which word is a relative pronoun?

hints.suum: 'Suum' is a possessive adjective meaning 'his own'

steps.prompt: What case is the relative pronoun 'qui'?

hints.ablative: Ablative of 'qui' would also be 'quo' (masc.) — here 'qui' is nominative, subject of 'seminat'
hints.dative: Dative would be 'cui' — 'qui' here is the nominative subject of the relative clause
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'cuius' — 'qui' here is the nominative subject

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'exit' — the doubled -i- in 'Exiit' marks the perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'exierit' — 'Exiit' is perfect indicative

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go) — 'exire' is its compound ex + ire
hints.exigo, exigere, exegi, exactum: These are the parts of 'exigere' (to demand/drive out)
hints.exerceo, exercere, exercui, exercitum: These are the parts of 'exercere' (to train/exercise)

hints.to plant: That would be 'plantare' — 'seminat' comes from 'seminare' (to sow)
hints.to scatter: That would be 'spargere' — 'seminat' comes from 'seminare' (to sow)
hints.to harvest: That would be 'metere' — 'seminat' comes from 'seminare' (to sow)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'seminet' — 'seminat' with -a- is present indicative (1st conj.)
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'seminavit' — 'seminat' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'seminabit' — 'seminat' is present indicative

hints.sero, serere, sevi, satum: These are the parts of 'serere' (to sow/plant), a different verb
hints.sino, sinere, sivi, situm: These are the parts of 'sinere' (to allow)
hints.sentio, sentire, sensi, sensum: These are the parts of 'sentire' (to feel/perceive)

hints.nominative: While 'semen' looks the same in nominative, here it is the object of 'seminare'
hints.ablative: Ablative of 'semen' would be 'semine' — here it is accusative, object of 'seminare'
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'seminis' — 'semen' is accusative here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'seminis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'seminis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'seminis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: While 'suum' could be nominative neuter, here it agrees with 'semen' as accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative neuter would be 'suo' — 'suum' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'sui' — 'suum' is accusative modifying 'semen'

translation: He who sows went out to sow his seed
